Central banks in adversarial or non-aligned nations have been diversifying reserves away from dollar assets, a trend accelerated by the weaponization of the financial system through sanctions. China, once among the largest foreign holders, has been steadily reducing its Treasury exposure. That leaves a narrower pool of buyers — predominantly allied governments in Europe and the Asia-Pacific — carrying a disproportionate share of the load.

The vulnerability is not merely theoretical. Treasury yields have already climbed as markets digest the scale of projected U.S. deficits, which are expected to remain above $1 trillion annually for the foreseeable future. Any further reduction in foreign demand would force either higher yields to attract domestic buyers, greater Federal Reserve intervention, or politically difficult spending restraint. None of those options is painless, and all would have ripple effects across global markets.

Analysts note the irony that the administration's own trade and foreign policy posture — including tariffs on allied nations and demands for increased NATO burden-sharing — could erode the goodwill that keeps allied central banks and sovereign wealth funds invested in American debt. The Treasury market, long considered the bedrock of global finance, now rests on a diplomatic foundation that looks less stable than at any point in recent memory.

articles.headline: Hillary Clinton Testifies Before House Panel in Epstein Investigation
articles.subheadline: Former secretary of state and her husband agreed to appear after facing potential contempt-of-Congress proceedings
articles.body:
Hillary Clinton appeared Thursday before the U.S. House Oversight Committee as part of its investigation into the crimes of Jeffrey Epstein, the convicted sex offender who died in federal custody in 2019. The former secretary of state and 2016 Democratic presidential nominee is providing testimony in Chappaqua, New York, near her home, with her husband, former President Bill Clinton, scheduled to follow on Friday.

The Clintons' decision to testify marked a significant reversal after months of resistance. Both had characterized the congressional subpoenas as politically motivated, calling them "nothing more than a ploy to attempt to embarrass political rivals, as President Trump has directed." Their change of course came as the committee moved toward a contempt-of-Congress vote — a proceeding that drew support from some Democratic members as well as Republicans.

Hillary Clinton has maintained that she has no recollection of ever meeting or speaking with Epstein. She did acknowledge meeting his convicted associate Ghislaine Maxwell "on a few occasions," including at the 2010 wedding of the Clintons' daughter Chelsea in New York. In a BBC interview last week from Berlin, she called the deposition a Republican strategy to deflect from questions about Donald Trump's own documented association with Epstein. "We have nothing to hide," she said. "We have called for the full release of these files repeatedly. We think sunlight is the best disinfectant."

Bill Clinton, who has acknowledged knowing Epstein but says he severed ties roughly two decades ago, has denied any wrongdoing or awareness of the financier's crimes. He appears in text messages and photographs contained in files the U.S. Department of Justice has been releasing in stages from federal investigations into Epstein. His Friday testimony would make him the first former U.S. president to appear before a congressional panel since Gerald Ford in 1983. As with other high-profile figures named in the files — including Trump — appearing in the documents does not imply wrongdoing.

The depositions are taking place behind closed doors, though the Clintons pushed successfully for their testimony to be made public, arguing that a private proceeding would invite selective leaks. Committee chair James Comer, a Republican, framed the effort as a bipartisan demonstration that "no-one is above the law." Members from both parties are expected to address reporters following the proceedings, with Hillary Clinton's session scheduled to begin at 11:00 a.m. Eastern.

articles.headline: Open Source Endowment Launches to Tackle Software's Chronic Funding Crisis
articles.subheadline: Backed by the former GitHub CEO, HashiCorp's founder, and 50+ donors, the nonprofit aims to reach $100 million in seven years
articles.body:
A newly formed nonprofit called the Open Source Endowment is attempting what decades of ad hoc donations and corporate sponsorships have not: creating a permanent, self-sustaining financial foundation for the developers who maintain the software underpinning the modern internet.

The initiative, which recently secured formal 501(c)(3) status, has raised more than $750,000 in commitments from over 50 donors. Its roster of backers reads like a who's who of the open-source world: Thomas Dohmke, the former GitHub CEO who recently raised $60 million for his developer tool startup Entire; Mitchell Hashimoto, whose HashiCorp sold to IBM for $6.4 billion; Supabase CEO Paul Copplestone; a co-founder of NGINX; and the creators of Vue.js and cURL, among executives from Elastic and Spotify. Founder Konstantin Vinogradov, a venture investor formerly at Runa Capital who specializes in open-source and infrastructure software, has set an ambitious target of growing the endowment to $100 million within seven years.

The urgency is real. Open-source software constitutes up to 55 percent of the technology stack in organizations worldwide, yet an estimated 86 percent of open-source developers receive no compensation for their work. The fragility of this arrangement was laid bare during the 2014 Heartbleed vulnerability, when a critical security flaw was discovered in OpenSSL — a project used across most of the internet but maintained by a single developer. Burnout among volunteer maintainers remains widespread, and existing funding mechanisms have proven insufficient. The Linux Foundation's Alpha-Omega Project distributed $5.8 million to just 14 projects in 2025, while corporate donations — such as Anthropic's $1.5 million gift to the Python Software Foundation in January — often amount to rounding errors for their donors. Anthropic itself raised $30 billion this month.

Vinogradov's model draws from the playbook of university endowments, which invest the bulk of their assets and spend only a fraction of returns each year. The approach demands patience — potentially years or decades to reach meaningful scale — but promises independence from the corporate influence that has generated controversy in open-source communities. The endowment plans to prioritize projects based on user base, downstream dependencies, and the absence of existing financial support. "The only way to support open source sustainably is private funds," Vinogradov told reporters.

Whether $750,000 in seed commitments can grow into a fund large enough to meaningfully sustain critical infrastructure remains an open question. But the caliber of the endowment's early supporters suggests the open-source community is betting that a slow, disciplined approach may succeed where faster fixes have fallen short.

articles.headline: Intellexa Founder Sentenced to Eight Years in Greek Spyware Case
articles.subheadline: Tal Dilian and three executives convicted in 'Greek Watergate' scandal involving surveillance of politicians and journalists
articles.body:
A Greek court has sentenced Tal Dilian, the founder of spyware consortium Intellexa, to eight years in prison for illegal wiretapping and privacy violations stemming from one of Europe's most consequential surveillance scandals.

The Thursday ruling also convicted three other Intellexa executives: Sara Aleksandra Fayssal Hamou, Dilian's business partner; Felix Bitzios, a former deputy administrator and shareholder; and Yiannis Lavranos, who owned a company with ties to Intellexa. The case, widely referred to as "Greek Watergate," centers on revelations dating to 2022 that the Greek government deployed Intellexa's Predator spyware to monitor the phones of politicians, journalists, businesspeople, and military officials — a sweeping domestic surveillance operation that triggered a national crisis.

The sentence represents one of the most significant legal consequences yet for the commercial spyware industry, which has faced mounting scrutiny from governments and civil liberties organizations worldwide. Intellexa's Predator tool has been linked to surveillance operations across multiple countries, drawing particular attention from the United States. In 2024, the U.S. Treasury Department imposed sanctions on Intellexa, several affiliated companies, Dilian, and Hamou for their role in developing spyware used to target American citizens, including government officials and journalists.

Despite the gravity of the conviction, the prison sentence has been stayed pending appeal, and the court ordered authorities to conduct further investigation into the broader surveillance operation. Dilian has not publicly responded to the ruling. The appeal process could take years to resolve under the Greek legal system, leaving open the question of whether the Intellexa founder will ultimately serve time behind bars.

The case underscores the growing willingness of European courts to hold spyware developers criminally accountable — a sharp departure from the near-total impunity the industry has historically enjoyed. It also highlights unresolved questions about government clients who purchased and deployed these tools. While Intellexa's makers now face prison, the Greek officials who authorized and directed the surveillance have largely avoided comparable legal consequences, a disparity that critics say leaves the deeper accountability gap unaddressed.

articles.headline: Blockchain Investigator Alleges Axiom Exchange Employee Exploited User Data for Insider Trading
articles.subheadline: ZachXBT claims a senior Axiom staffer tracked private wallets to front-run memecoin trades, raising fresh concerns about centralized platform trust
articles.body:
A prominent blockchain investigator known as ZachXBT has accused a senior employee at onchain trading platform Axiom Exchange of abusing internal access to user data in order to track private wallets and trade memecoins using inside information.

In a detailed thread posted to X, ZachXBT identified Broox Bauer, a New York-based senior business development employee at Axiom, as the individual at the center of the allegations. According to the investigator, Bauer used internal dashboards to look up sensitive user information — including linked wallet addresses — and shared that data with a small group that mapped the trading activity of prominent crypto influencers. Audio clips shared in the thread purportedly capture Bauer claiming he could track "any Axiom user" by referral code, wallet address, or user ID, and "find out anything to do with that person." In the same recording, the speaker describes gradually escalating research from 10–20 wallets to broader activity "so it does not look that suspicious."

Axiom Exchange, founded in 2024 by individuals known as Mist and Cal, is a member of Y Combinator's Winter 2025 cohort and has generated more than $390 million in revenue to date, according to ZachXBT's findings. The platform facilitates onchain trading and has built a significant user base in the memecoin and decentralized finance sectors. ZachXBT stated he was retained to investigate allegations that internal tools were being misused, though he did not disclose who hired him.

The accusations land at a sensitive moment for crypto infrastructure providers. As centralized platforms increasingly serve as gateways to decentralized markets, the trust users place in these intermediaries becomes a critical vulnerability. Unlike traditional brokerages subject to strict regulatory oversight on the handling of client data, many crypto trading platforms operate with fewer formal guardrails. If the allegations prove accurate, the case would represent a clear example of how privileged access at a platform level can be weaponized against unsuspecting users — a scenario that echoes longstanding concerns about front-running and information asymmetry in digital asset markets.

Neither Axiom Exchange nor Bauer has publicly responded to the allegations as of publication. The case underscores the growing role of independent blockchain investigators like ZachXBT, whose open-source forensic work has become a de facto accountability mechanism in an industry where formal enforcement often lags behind the pace of misconduct.

articles.headline: Figma Integrates OpenAI's Codex, Deepening AI Design-to-Code Push
articles.subheadline: The partnership follows a similar Anthropic deal by just one week, as Figma races to bridge design and engineering workflows
articles.body:
Figma announced on Wednesday that it has integrated OpenAI's Codex AI coding tool into its platform, allowing users to seamlessly move between visual design and code implementation. The partnership marks the design company's second major AI integration in a week, following a similar deal with Anthropic's Claude Code.

The integration leverages Figma's Model Context Protocol (MCP) server to enable fluid transitions between the two platforms. Users can begin work in either Figma's design environment or OpenAI's Codex coding tool and shift between the two without disrupting their workflow. Previously, Figma users could bring design file details into Codex for code-based implementation, but the new integration promises a more bidirectional experience. "Engineers can iterate visually without leaving their flow, and designers can work closer to real implementation without becoming full-time coders," said Alexander Embiricos, Codex product lead at OpenAI.

The timing underscores the intensifying competition between OpenAI and Anthropic for developer ecosystem dominance. OpenAI originally launched Codex as a command-line coding assistant to rival Anthropic's well-regarded Claude Code. The tool has since been embedded into ChatGPT, and a dedicated macOS app released earlier this month was downloaded more than one million times in its first week. OpenAI says Codex now has over one million weekly active users, a figure bolstered by the recent release of two new Codex models.

For Figma, the rapid-fire AI partnerships signal a strategic bet that the future of design tooling lies in collapsing the boundary between visual creation and software engineering. Chief Design Officer Loredan Crisan framed the integration as enabling teams to "build on their best ideas—not just their first idea" by combining code with the collaborative creativity of Figma's canvas. The company has been a prominent OpenAI partner since it became one of the first to launch an app within ChatGPT in October 2025.

The back-to-back deals with both leading AI coding platforms position Figma as an agnostic bridge between design and development, regardless of which AI assistant a team prefers. As AI-powered coding tools mature and their user bases grow into the millions, design platforms like Figma appear to be positioning themselves not merely as creative tools but as essential nodes in an increasingly automated software development pipeline.

articles.headline: Ethereum Unveils Sweeping Roadmap: Seven Hard Forks by 2029
articles.subheadline: The 'strawmap' targets 8-second transaction finality, quantum-resistant cryptography, and built-in privacy features
articles.body:
The Ethereum Foundation on Wednesday published what may be its most ambitious technical blueprint in years — a detailed plan for seven hard forks through the end of the decade that would fundamentally reshape how the world's largest smart contract platform operates.

The document, dubbed the "strawmap" and authored by Ethereum Foundation researcher Justin Drake, sets out five overarching objectives: compressing transaction finality from roughly 16 minutes to as few as 8 seconds; scaling the base layer to approximately 10,000 transactions per second; enabling Layer 2 networks to reach throughput on the order of 10 million transactions per second; deploying post-quantum cryptography; and introducing shielded ETH transfers for user privacy. The finality improvement alone — a roughly 480-fold reduction — would be achieved through a new consensus mechanism called Minimmit, which reaches agreement in a single voting round rather than the multiple rounds required today.

Ethereum co-founder Vitalik Buterin endorsed the plan as "very important" and described how the network's block slot time, currently fixed at 12 seconds, would be dialed down incrementally — from 12 to 8 to 6 to 4 and potentially to 2 seconds — with each step contingent on demonstrated network safety. Buterin characterized the broader approach as a "ship of Theseus" rebuild, in which individual consensus components are swapped out piece by piece until the system is effectively new, without any single upgrade being excessively disruptive.

Two features in the roadmap carry particular strategic significance. The post-quantum cryptography initiative would replace Ethereum's current elliptic-curve signatures with hash-based schemes resistant to attacks from sufficiently powerful quantum computers. While some industry figures — including Strategy's Michael Saylor — have dismissed quantum threats as more than a decade away, Ethereum's plan treats the risk as a concrete engineering challenge with a specific fork target. Meanwhile, shielded transfers would allow users to send ETH without exposing transaction amounts, senders, or recipients on the public blockchain, addressing long-standing privacy concerns that have driven some users toward alternative networks.

The roadmap lands at a moment of notable tension between Ethereum's technical ambitions and its market performance. Ether was trading near $2,028 at the time of publication, up roughly 7% on the day but still well below its historical highs. Whether these sweeping infrastructure promises can revive broader confidence in the asset — or whether subdued sentiment will overshadow the engineering progress — remains the central question for Ethereum stakeholders heading into the second half of 2026.

articles.headline: eBay Cuts 800 Jobs Despite Strong Revenue Growth
articles.subheadline: The e-commerce giant trims 6% of its workforce just days after announcing a $1.2 billion acquisition of Depop
articles.body:
eBay announced Wednesday that it will eliminate approximately 800 positions, representing roughly 6% of its full-time workforce, as the company restructures to align with what it described as evolving strategic priorities. The cuts mark the third round of significant layoffs at the online marketplace in just three years.

The timing is notable. eBay's move comes barely a week after the company revealed plans to acquire Depop, the Gen Z-favored secondhand fashion marketplace, from Etsy in a $1.2 billion all-cash deal. It also follows a strong fourth-quarter earnings report in which revenue climbed 15% year over year to $3 billion, surpassing Wall Street estimates. The juxtaposition of robust financial performance, aggressive deal-making, and workforce reductions underscores a broader pattern across the tech industry, where companies increasingly frame layoffs not as responses to distress but as strategic reallocations of resources.

In a statement, eBay said it is "taking steps to reinvest across our business and align our structure with our strategic priorities," adding that it would support affected employees "with care and respect." The company did not specify which divisions or geographies would bear the brunt of the reductions. Bloomberg first reported the layoffs.

The job cuts continue a pattern that has become almost annual at eBay. In early 2024, the company eliminated roughly 1,000 roles — about 9% of its headcount at the time. A year before that, in early 2023, it cut approximately 500 positions, or 4% of its workforce. Cumulatively, eBay has shed well over 2,000 jobs since 2023, even as its top line has strengthened.

The Depop acquisition signals that eBay is betting on younger demographics and the booming resale economy as key growth vectors. Integrating a consumer brand with a distinct identity and user base, however, will require significant organizational effort — a reality that may partly explain why the company is simultaneously trimming existing roles. For eBay, the message to investors appears clear: growth will come from targeted bets on new markets, not from expanding headcount across legacy operations.

articles.headline: Instagram to Notify Parents When Teens Search Suicide Content
articles.subheadline: New parental alerts roll out amid mounting lawsuits accusing Meta of failing to protect young users
articles.body:
Meta's Instagram is introducing a new safety feature that will alert parents when their teenager repeatedly searches for terms related to suicide or self-harm, the company announced on Thursday. The notifications will begin rolling out in the coming weeks across the United States, United Kingdom, Australia, and Canada, with other regions to follow later this year.

The alerts will be sent via email, text, or WhatsApp to parents enrolled in Instagram's parental supervision tools, along with an in-app notification containing resources to help guide conversations with their children. Triggers include phrases encouraging self-harm, language suggesting a teen may be at risk, and direct search terms such as "suicide" or "self-harm." Instagram already blocks users from viewing such content in search results, but the new feature is designed to ensure parents are informed when their child is actively seeking it out.

The timing of the announcement is notable. Meta is currently embroiled in multiple lawsuits alleging its platforms have harmed minors. This week, Instagram head Adam Mosseri faced sharp questioning from prosecutors in a U.S. District Court case in Northern California over what plaintiffs characterize as delayed deployment of basic safety features, including a nudity filter for private messages sent to teens. In a separate proceeding before the Los Angeles County Superior Court, internal Meta research was disclosed showing that the company's own parental supervision tools had little measurable impact on children's compulsive social media use — a finding that could undercut the very category of feature Meta is now expanding.

Instagram said it calibrated the alert threshold in consultation with its Suicide and Self-Harm Advisory Group, choosing to err on the side of caution even if that means some parents receive notifications when no genuine risk exists. "We analyzed Instagram search behavior and consulted with experts," the company wrote in a blog post, adding that it would "continue to monitor and listen to feedback." The platform also disclosed plans to eventually extend similar notifications to cases where a teen attempts to engage Instagram's AI tools in conversations about self-harm.

The feature represents Meta's latest effort to demonstrate proactive safety measures at a moment when regulatory and legal pressure on social media companies over teen mental health has reached an inflection point. Whether parental alerts meaningfully change outcomes — particularly given Meta's own research questioning the efficacy of its supervision tools — remains an open question that courts and policymakers are likely to scrutinize closely.

articles.headline: Jersey Approves Assisted Dying Law in Landmark British Isles Vote
articles.subheadline: The Crown dependency becomes the second jurisdiction in the British Isles to legalize assisted dying, with first legal deaths possible by summer 2026
articles.body:
Jersey's parliament voted 32 to 16 on Thursday to approve a law allowing terminally ill adults to choose to end their own lives, making the Channel Island the second jurisdiction in the British Isles — after the Isle of Man — to fully pass such legislation. The bill now awaits Royal Assent from the UK, considered a formality, after which the first legal assisted deaths could occur as early as next summer.

Under the new law, eligibility is restricted to adults with terminal illnesses causing unbearable suffering who are expected to die within six months, or within 12 months for those with neurodegenerative conditions such as Parkinson's disease or motor neurone disease. Applicants must have been resident in Jersey for at least 12 months. Tom Binet, the island's Minister for Health and Social Services, said Jersey would now have "one of the safest and most transparent assisted dying laws in the world."

The vote was met with celebration outside the States Chamber by campaigners who have long pushed for the reform. Lorna Pirozzolo, a Jersey resident with terminal breast cancer, called the law "badly needed" and said Thursday's decision brought "enormous relief, not just for me, but for future generations of islanders who deserve compassion, choice and dignity at the end of life." Humanists UK chief executive Andrew Copson described the vote as a "momentous vote of confidence" and noted the legislation contains strong safeguards reflecting public sentiment expressed through citizens' juries and surveys.

Opponents voiced deep reservations. Deputy Sir Philip Bailhache, who voted against the measure, said he feared it would "change the community of Jersey in a way in which we may not yet be able to foresee," arguing that "life is a precious thing" and raising unresolved ethical concerns about authorizing the deliberate ending of life.

The decision carries broader implications across the British Isles, where assisted dying remains one of the most intensely debated social issues. Westminster and the Scottish Parliament are both considering their own legislation, though the bill for England and Wales has made slow progress in the House of Lords. Labour peer Lord Falconer, who is sponsoring the UK bill, said the Jersey law was "quite similar" to the proposals under debate at Westminster and that both Jersey and the Isle of Man reflect a strong public consensus that current laws are "so cruel." A UK government spokesperson noted that the timeline for processing such legislation depends on its "complexities and sensitivities." Jersey's swift passage could intensify pressure on lawmakers in London and Edinburgh to advance their own reforms.

articles.headline: WHO Chief Condemns US Decision to Cut mRNA Research Funding
articles.subheadline: Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus calls the move a 'wrong decision,' raising concerns about future pandemic preparedness
articles.body:
World Health Organization Director-General Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus has publicly criticized the United States for withdrawing funding from mRNA research, calling the decision a mistake that could undermine global health preparedness.

Speaking to Bloomberg's Mishal Husain on February 26, Tedros described the defunding as "a wrong decision," pointing to the central role mRNA technology played in developing the Covid-19 vaccines that helped bring the pandemic under control. The platform, which enabled companies like Pfizer-BioNTech and Moderna to produce effective vaccines at unprecedented speed, is widely regarded as one of the most significant biomedical advances of the past decade — and researchers have been exploring its potential applications in cancer treatment, influenza vaccines, and preparedness for future pandemics.

The US pullback on mRNA funding comes amid broader cuts to health and scientific research programs under the current administration. The National Institutes of Health and other federal agencies have faced significant budget reductions, with critics warning that reduced investment in foundational biomedical research could erode American leadership in the life sciences and leave the world less equipped to respond to emerging infectious diseases. Proponents of the cuts have argued that fiscal restraint is necessary and that the private sector can fill gaps left by reduced government spending.

Tedros's remarks carry particular weight given the WHO's role in coordinating global health responses and its front-row view of how mRNA technology reshaped the trajectory of the Covid-19 crisis. The organization has been working to expand mRNA vaccine manufacturing capacity in low- and middle-income countries, an effort that could be complicated if upstream research loses momentum due to the withdrawal of its largest funder.

The criticism from the WHO chief adds to a growing chorus of concern from scientists, public health officials, and pharmaceutical industry leaders who warn that abandoning investment in mRNA research risks squandering a hard-won technological advantage — one that was built on decades of publicly funded basic science and proved its worth during the deadliest pandemic in a century.

articles.headline: Indiana Passes Bill Opening Public Pension Funds to Bitcoin and Crypto ETFs
articles.subheadline: The state becomes the latest to embrace digital assets for retirement plans while simultaneously banning crypto ATMs over fraud concerns
articles.body:
Indiana's state legislature has approved a bill that would allow public retirement and savings plans to invest in digital assets and spot cryptocurrency exchange-traded funds, adding the state to a rapidly growing cohort embracing crypto for public finance. Governor Mike Braun is expected to sign HB 1042 into law within ten days.

The move places Indiana among at least eight states — including Wyoming, Wisconsin, Michigan, and Arizona — that have formally integrated crypto-linked products into public investment frameworks. According to a CoinDesk analysis, a total of 21 states are now either actively investing in or evaluating digital asset allocations, primarily in bitcoin and, in some cases, dollar-pegged stablecoins. Much of the momentum has built since former President Donald Trump directed his administration to establish a Bitcoin Strategic Reserve and pledged to make the United States the "crypto capital of the world." States such as Arizona, Tennessee, Oklahoma, and Nebraska have already signed legislation opening certain public funds to cryptocurrency purchases.

Yet Indiana's embrace of crypto is not without caveats. In a separate measure passed the same day, state legislators voted to ban crypto ATMs — also known as virtual currency kiosks — statewide, citing an alarming rise in consumer fraud. Violations would be enforceable by the state attorney general under deceptive consumer sales laws. In Evansville, Indiana, alone, authorities reported approximately $400,000 in scam losses connected to the kiosks during 2025. The FBI has estimated that Americans lost $240 million to crypto ATM fraud in just the first half of 2025, and the agency received nearly 11,000 related complaints in 2024, a 99 percent increase over the prior year. In a notable parallel action, the Massachusetts attorney general has filed a lawsuit against ATM operator Bitcoin Depot, alleging the company allowed criminals to exploit its machines to defraud consumers.

The dual legislative actions capture the complex posture many state governments are adopting toward cryptocurrency: eager to tap the potential upside of digital assets for investment portfolios while scrambling to contain the consumer protection risks that accompany the technology's rapid spread. For Indiana's public employees, the practical impact will depend on how fund administrators choose to allocate within the new legal framework — and whether the volatile asset class delivers returns that justify the political leap of placing retirement savings alongside bitcoin's price swings. Bitcoin was trading near $68,200 on Wednesday, down roughly 2.9 percent on the day.

articles.headline: Strategy's STRC Preferred Stock Draws Corporate Buyers as Shares Hit Par
articles.subheadline: Three firms disclosed treasury allocations to the bitcoin-linked preferred equity at Strategy World 2026 in Las Vegas
articles.body:
Michael Saylor's Strategy is seeing growing corporate adoption of its preferred stock instrument, with three companies revealing they have added the firm's Stretch (STRC) perpetual preferred equity to their balance sheets just as the security returned to its $100 par value.

Prevalon Energy, crypto custodian Anchorage Digital, and Brazilian bitcoin treasury firm OranjeBTC each disclosed allocations to STRC during presentations at Strategy World 2026, the company's annual conference in Las Vegas. The announcements came during the event's "Bitcoin for Corporations" track, which has become a focal point for companies exploring bitcoin-linked treasury strategies. STRC — formally known as the Variable Rate Series A Perpetual Stretch Preferred Stock — is a short-duration, high-yield credit instrument that ranks senior to Strategy's common stock and pays an 11.25% annual dividend distributed monthly.

The timing of the disclosures coincided with a notable market milestone: STRC briefly touched its $100 par value during Wednesday's trading session, according to tracking site STRC.live, and returned to that level in pre-market trading Thursday. Trading activity around the par recovery was estimated to have driven the equivalent of roughly 22 bitcoin in purchases through STRC-related flows.

Beyond direct corporate treasury adoption, Strategy's preferred stock is also gaining international distribution. Asset manager 21Shares announced at the conference that it would bring STRC exposure to European investors through a Strategy Yield exchange-traded product listed on Euronext Amsterdam. The move extends the reach of Saylor's bitcoin-proxy financial instruments beyond U.S. markets for the first time.

The conference also featured a significant disclosure from traditional finance: Morgan Stanley's Amy Oldenburg, head of digital asset strategy at the bank, confirmed during a panel with Strategy CEO Phong Le that Morgan Stanley plans to introduce bitcoin trading, lending, yield, and custody services. Strategy's common shares rose 9% on Wednesday to around $135, while bitcoin traded above $68,000. The broadening institutional embrace of both STRC and bitcoin-adjacent financial products suggests that Saylor's long-running campaign to position Strategy as the primary corporate conduit to bitcoin exposure continues to gain traction, even as the cryptocurrency itself remains range-bound well below its all-time highs.

articles.headline: Oil Supertanker Rates Surge 600%, Squeezing Producers and Refiners
articles.subheadline: Soaring shipping costs for very large crude carriers are reshaping the economics of global oil trade
articles.body:
Rates for the world's largest oil tankers have skyrocketed by roughly 600%, transforming supertankers into one of the most sought-after commodities in global energy markets and raising alarm among oil producers and refiners already navigating a volatile pricing environment.

The extraordinary surge in chartering costs for very large crude carriers — the massive vessels capable of transporting approximately two million barrels of oil per voyage — reflects a confluence of tightening vessel supply, shifting trade routes, and sustained demand for seaborne crude. The spike has added significant per-barrel transportation costs that are now substantial enough to erode margins for both upstream producers and downstream refiners, according to Bloomberg reporting.

The rally in supertanker rates underscores how logistics bottlenecks can ripple through the entire petroleum supply chain. When shipping costs climb this steeply, the economics of long-haul crude shipments — particularly from the Middle East and West Africa to refineries in Asia — shift materially. Refiners who depend on delivered crude pricing find their feedstock costs inflated, while producers in export-heavy regions may face pressure to discount their barrels to remain competitive. The dynamic effectively acts as a hidden tax on global oil flows, one that rarely garners the same attention as headline crude benchmarks but can be equally consequential for industry profitability.

Several structural factors have contributed to the tightness in the tanker market. An aging global fleet, limited new vessel orders placed during years of subdued returns, and longer voyage distances resulting from sanctions-driven rerouting of Russian crude have all reduced the effective supply of available ships. At the same time, OPEC+ production decisions and resilient Asian demand have kept crude volumes moving across oceans at a steady clip, intensifying competition for scarce tonnage.

For energy traders and market watchers, the supertanker boom serves as a reminder that the oil market is not merely a function of barrels produced and consumed — it is a sprawling logistical network where freight rates, port congestion, and fleet availability can move billions of dollars in value. Whether the current rate environment proves sustainable or triggers demand destruction for long-haul shipments remains one of the key questions for commodity markets in the months ahead.

articles.headline: Nvidia Crushes Q4 Estimates With $68.1B Revenue, Guides Higher
articles.subheadline: Data center revenue surged 75% year-over-year as CEO Jensen Huang declares AI demand will only accelerate
articles.body:
Nvidia delivered another blockbuster quarter on Wednesday, reporting fourth-quarter revenue of $68.1 billion — a 73% jump from a year earlier and roughly $2 billion above Wall Street's consensus estimate of $66.1 billion. Adjusted earnings per share came in at $1.62, also topping the $1.54 analysts had projected, according to FactSet data.

The results were impressive, but Nvidia's forward guidance may have been even more striking. The chipmaker told investors to expect first-quarter revenue of approximately $78 billion, blowing past the $72.9 billion analysts had penciled in. The outlook signals that the torrent of AI infrastructure spending shows no sign of abating, despite periodic market anxieties about the durability of the boom. Shares climbed as much as 4% in after-hours trading before settling to roughly 1% higher.

Nvidia's data center segment — now the overwhelming engine of the company's growth — posted record quarterly revenue of $62.3 billion, up 75% year-over-year and 22% sequentially. CFO Colette Kress noted during the earnings call that the division has scaled by nearly 13 times since the emergence of ChatGPT, and that inventory and supply commitments are already in place to meet demand extending into 2027. "Today's report is a strong pushback against the narrative that hyperscaler AI growth could start fading into 2027," said Thomas Monteiro, senior analyst at Investing.com.

CEO Jensen Huang struck a characteristically bullish tone, framing current computational demand as a fraction of what lies ahead. "AI is here. AI is not going to go back. AI is only going to get better from here," he said. The remarks underscore Nvidia's position at the center of a capital expenditure cycle in which the world's largest cloud providers — Microsoft, Amazon, Google, and Meta among them — continue to pour tens of billions of dollars into GPU-powered data centers.

The ripple effects extended beyond Nvidia's own stock. Bitcoin held near $69,000 following a sharp recovery earlier in the week, while AI-adjacent crypto tokens including Bittensor and Internet Computer posted modest gains. Crypto mining companies with exposure to high-performance computing infrastructure, such as IREN, Cipher Digital, and TeraWulf, also ticked higher in after-hours trading before giving back some of those moves. For markets broadly, the quarter reinforced a simple thesis: the AI infrastructure buildout remains firmly in expansion mode, and Nvidia sits squarely at its center.

articles.headline: Nvidia Forecast Fails to Wow as Cuba Incident Rattles Markets
articles.subheadline: AI chipmaker's sales outlook underwhelms investors while a deadly Cuba speedboat shooting raises geopolitical tensions with Washington
articles.body:
Global markets opened under a cloud on Wednesday as two unrelated developments — a tepid earnings outlook from Nvidia and a deadly confrontation off the Cuban coast — combined to dampen investor sentiment across European and pre-market U.S. trading.

Nvidia, the world's dominant supplier of artificial intelligence processors, delivered a sales forecast that fell short of the lofty expectations baked into its valuation. While the company continues to benefit from surging demand for AI chips, the guidance signaled that investor anxiety over an overheated AI economy is far from abating. The stock, which has been a bellwether for the broader AI trade, faced selling pressure as traders reassessed whether the sector's blistering growth trajectory can sustain current multiples. The reaction underscored a growing wariness on Wall Street that AI spending may be approaching a plateau, or at minimum a period of more measured expansion.

Meanwhile, a geopolitical flashpoint emerged in the Caribbean after Cuban forces killed four individuals who allegedly opened fire from a speedboat bearing Florida license tags. U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio confirmed that Washington is investigating the incident, which carries the potential to escalate an already fraught standoff between the United States and Cuba. The circumstances of the confrontation — particularly the Florida connection — inject an unpredictable variable into U.S.-Cuba relations at a time when the diplomatic relationship is already under strain.

The convergence of a disappointing Big Tech earnings signal and a fresh geopolitical risk factor set the tone for cautious trading across European exchanges. Analysts noted that Nvidia's results carry outsized importance for broader market direction given the chipmaker's enormous weighting in major indices and its role as a proxy for AI investment sentiment. Any sign that the AI boom is cooling tends to reverberate well beyond the semiconductor sector.

Investors will be watching closely in the coming sessions for further details on both fronts — whether Nvidia can offer reassurance on long-term demand trends, and whether the Cuba incident remains contained or develops into a broader diplomatic crisis between Washington and Havana.

articles.headline: Nvidia Posts $68B Record Quarter as AI Demand Goes 'Exponential'
articles.subheadline: The world's most valuable company sees data center revenue surge to $62 billion while eyeing OpenAI investment
articles.body:
Nvidia delivered yet another record-breaking quarter on Wednesday, reporting $68 billion in revenue for its fiscal quarter ending in early 2026 — a 73 percent jump from the same period a year earlier — as insatiable demand for artificial intelligence computing continues to drive the chipmaker's extraordinary growth trajectory.

The data center division, which has become the overwhelming engine of Nvidia's business, accounted for $62 billion of the total, with the company for the first time breaking out compute revenue ($51 billion, largely from GPU sales) from networking products like NVLink ($11 billion). Full-year revenue reached $215 billion. CEO Jensen Huang struck a bullish tone on the earnings call, declaring that "the demand for tokens in the world has gone completely exponential" and noting that even six-year-old Nvidia GPUs deployed in cloud data centers are fully utilized with rising prices.

Huang moved to quell growing investor anxiety about the sustainability of massive capital expenditure commitments by major technology companies, framing AI compute as a direct revenue driver. "In this new world of AI, compute is revenue. Without compute, there's no way to generate tokens. Without tokens, there's no way to grow revenues," he said, adding that the industry has reached an "inflection point" where token generation is now profitable for cloud service providers.

The quarter also surfaced notable developments on the geopolitical and strategic fronts. Despite the recent easing of U.S. export restrictions, Nvidia reported zero revenue from China chip sales. Chief Financial Officer Colette Kress disclosed that while small quantities of H200 chips had been approved for Chinese customers, no shipments have generated revenue, and the company remains uncertain whether imports will ultimately be permitted. Kress also acknowledged that Chinese competitors, buoyed by recent IPOs such as Moore Threads' December listing, pose a potential long-term threat to the global AI chip landscape.

On the deal-making front, Huang confirmed the company is nearing a partnership agreement with OpenAI, with a reported investment of $30 billion under discussion. He also cited partnerships with Anthropic, Meta, and Elon Musk's xAI. However, Nvidia's SEC filing tempered expectations, noting there is "no assurance" the OpenAI investment will materialize. The disclosures underscore both Nvidia's dominance in the AI infrastructure stack and the complex web of competitive and regulatory pressures shaping the chip industry's future.

articles.headline: Syngenta Revives Hong Kong IPO Bid Amid Market Rally
articles.subheadline: Chinese-owned agrochemical giant eyes one of the city's largest public offerings after previous listing attempt collapsed
articles.body:
Syngenta Group is preparing a fresh attempt to list on the Hong Kong stock exchange, positioning what could become one of the city's biggest initial public offerings in recent years as Asian equity markets enjoy a sustained rally.

The renewed push marks a significant reversal for the Swiss-founded agrochemical and seed giant, which is owned by China National Chemical Corporation (ChemChina), itself part of state-owned conglomerate Sinochem. Syngenta previously pursued a blockbuster Shanghai listing that was expected to raise roughly $9 billion before the company abruptly withdrew its application in 2024, citing market conditions and strategic considerations. That collapse was one of the most high-profile IPO failures in recent Chinese capital markets history.

The decision to target Hong Kong rather than Shanghai reflects a shifting calculus for major Chinese-linked enterprises seeking access to international capital pools. Hong Kong's market has staged a notable recovery in recent months, buoyed by renewed investor enthusiasm for Chinese technology stocks and a broader thaw in sentiment toward Chinese assets. The Hang Seng Index has climbed significantly from its 2024 lows, creating a more favorable window for large-scale offerings. Several major listings have tested the waters in the city recently, suggesting the IPO pipeline is reopening after a prolonged drought.

Syngenta, headquartered in Basel, Switzerland, is one of the world's largest agricultural companies, with operations spanning crop protection chemicals, seeds, and digital farming technologies across more than 100 countries. ChemChina acquired the company in 2017 for approximately $43 billion in what was then the largest-ever Chinese overseas acquisition. A successful public listing would give Sinochem a mechanism to unlock some of that investment value while providing Syngenta with a public currency for future deals and growth initiatives.

The precise size and timeline of the offering have not been finalized, and market conditions could still shift before shares begin trading. However, the scale of Syngenta's global operations suggests the IPO could raise several billion dollars, which would represent a major vote of confidence in Hong Kong's ability to attract heavyweight listings back to the city after years of competition from other financial centers.

articles.headline: Mexico Engulfed in Cartel Violence Across 20 States After Drug Lord's Death
articles.subheadline: The Jalisco New Generation Cartel has unleashed widespread attacks following the capture and death of leader 'El Mencho'
articles.body:
Mexico is experiencing a devastating surge in cartel violence spanning 20 states after the death of Nemesio Oseguera Cervantes, the feared leader of the Jalisco New Generation Cartel (CJNG) known as "El Mencho," who died shortly after being captured in a bloody firefight in the western state of Jalisco.

Members of the CJNG — one of Mexico's most powerful criminal organizations — have transformed towns and cities across the country into active war zones in apparent retaliation for their leader's capture. The wave of violence represents one of the most geographically widespread cartel escalations in recent memory, with attacks reported across a vast swath of Mexican territory.

The crisis is compounding an already dire security situation in regions like Culiacán, the capital of the northern state of Sinaloa, which has been mired in its own spiral of internecine violence since the 2024 removal of Sinaloa Cartel leader Ismael "El Mayo" Zambada. Zambada, who led the cartel for decades, is now imprisoned in the United States. BBC international correspondent Quentin Sommerville, reporting from Culiacán's streets, embedded with two local paramedics — Julio César Vega and Héctor Torres — who respond daily to shootings and stabbings. Torres told the BBC that the violence in Culiacán has reached unprecedented levels in both intensity and duration.

The simultaneous destabilization of Mexico's two most powerful drug trafficking organizations — the CJNG and the Sinaloa Cartel — has created a security vacuum that analysts have long warned could trigger exactly this kind of cascading conflict. With both groups fractured or in turmoil, rival factions and smaller organizations may be seizing the opportunity to contest territory, fueling violence that is increasingly affecting ordinary civilians and the first responders who serve them.

The crisis poses a significant challenge for Mexico's government, which faces mounting pressure to restore order across a country where cartel influence has become deeply entrenched. For residents of cities like Culiacán, the paramedics navigating blood-soaked streets offer a grim window into daily life in a nation where the consequences of the drug trade continue to exact an extraordinary human toll.

articles.headline: Top Asset Managers Overseeing $20T Turn Bullish on Emerging Markets
articles.subheadline: Citigroup survey finds the world's biggest money managers favor EM equities, currencies, bonds, and credit
articles.body:
The world's largest asset managers, collectively responsible for more than $20 trillion in assets, have tilted decisively toward emerging markets across virtually every asset class, according to Citigroup.

The broad-based optimism spans emerging-market equities, currencies, local-currency bonds, and credit — a rare alignment of bullish sentiment that suggests major institutional investors see a favorable setup for developing economies in 2026. The findings, reported by Bloomberg on Wednesday, signal a potentially significant shift in global capital flows at a time when investors are reassessing the relative attractiveness of developed and emerging markets.

The sweeping nature of the positioning is notable. Rather than selectively favoring one corner of the emerging-market universe, the largest allocators appear to be making a comprehensive bet on the asset class. Such broad conviction across equities, fixed income, and currencies simultaneously often reflects expectations of improving macroeconomic fundamentals, attractive valuations relative to developed markets, or both.

Emerging markets have historically benefited from periods of dollar weakness, favorable commodity prices, and divergent monetary policy cycles — conditions that can draw yield-hungry institutional capital away from lower-returning developed-market assets. The scale of the managers surveyed — with combined assets exceeding $20 trillion — means even modest allocation shifts could translate into substantial inflows for developing-economy assets.

The Citi findings come as investors globally navigate an uncertain landscape shaped by shifting trade policies, evolving central bank trajectories, and geopolitical realignments. For emerging markets, the endorsement from the world's most influential allocators could serve as both a signal and a catalyst, potentially reinforcing the very flows that make the bullish thesis self-fulfilling in the near term.

articles.headline: London Stock Exchange Group Announces £3 Billion Buyback Amid Elliott Pressure
articles.subheadline: LSEG unveils massive share repurchase program alongside full-year results, weeks after activist investor disclosed stake
articles.body:
The London Stock Exchange Group has unveiled a £3 billion ($4.1 billion) share buyback program, delivering a significant capital return to shareholders just weeks after activist investor Elliott Investment Management revealed it had built a position in the company.

The repurchase announcement came alongside LSEG's full-year earnings report and signals a willingness by the exchange operator's leadership to boost shareholder value at a moment of heightened scrutiny. Elliott, the hedge fund founded by Paul Singer that manages roughly $70 billion in assets, is known for aggressively pushing portfolio companies toward operational improvements, strategic shifts, and enhanced capital returns. The firm's stake in LSEG emerged in late January, setting the stage for potential engagement between the activist and the company's board.

LSEG has transformed dramatically over the past several years, evolving from a traditional exchange operator into a data and analytics powerhouse following its blockbuster $27 billion acquisition of Refinitiv in 2021. That deal, which brought vast financial data assets and the Workspace terminal platform under LSEG's umbrella, repositioned the company as a direct competitor to Bloomberg LP in the financial information services market. The company's shares have reflected that transformation, but Elliott's involvement suggests the activist sees further room for value creation — whether through margin expansion, additional buybacks, or strategic realignment.

The £3 billion buyback represents a substantial commitment relative to LSEG's market capitalization, which stands at roughly £60 billion. While the company has conducted share repurchases in the past, this program is notably larger and arrives at a politically significant moment. Whether the buyback was already in the pipeline or was accelerated in response to Elliott's presence remains unclear, but the timing underscores how activist engagement can catalyze corporate action even before formal demands are made.

For LSEG, the challenge ahead will be demonstrating that its post-Refinitiv integration is delivering sustainable growth while simultaneously returning capital at scale. Elliott's track record of extended campaigns at major European companies — including past engagements with GSK and Softbank — suggests the activist is unlikely to be satisfied by a single buyback announcement alone. Investors will be watching closely for any further demands from Elliott and how LSEG's board responds in the months ahead.

articles.headline: Shell Explores Minority Stake Sale in $24B Australian LNG Plant
articles.subheadline: Abu Dhabi's Adnoc among potential buyers as oil major weighs partial divestiture of North West Shelf facility
articles.body:
Shell Plc is in discussions with several companies, including a subsidiary of Abu Dhabi National Oil Co., about selling a minority stake in its massive North West Shelf liquefied natural gas export facility in Western Australia, according to people familiar with the negotiations.

The North West Shelf plant, valued at approximately A$34 billion ($24 billion), is one of Australia's most significant energy assets and a cornerstone of the country's LNG export infrastructure. A partial divestiture would represent a major transaction in the global energy sector, potentially reshaping the ownership structure of a facility that has been central to Asia-Pacific gas supply for decades.

The involvement of Adnoc — the state-owned oil company of the United Arab Emirates — signals the Gulf producer's continued appetite for downstream and international energy assets as it diversifies beyond its traditional role as a crude oil supplier. Adnoc has been on an aggressive expansion path in recent years, acquiring stakes in natural gas operations and petrochemical businesses across multiple continents.

For Shell, a stake sale would align with the London-based energy giant's broader portfolio optimization strategy, allowing it to unlock capital from mature assets while retaining operational involvement. The company has been selectively pruning its global holdings to concentrate investment where it sees the highest returns, and partial divestments of large-scale infrastructure have been a recurring feature of that approach.

The talks remain ongoing and there is no certainty that a deal will be reached. The identities of other parties involved in the discussions beyond Adnoc have not been disclosed. Any transaction of this scale would likely face regulatory scrutiny from Australian authorities, given the strategic importance of LNG exports to the national economy and energy security landscape.

articles.headline: Rolls-Royce Announces Record Share Buyback Amid Surging Engine Demand
articles.subheadline: Britain's largest manufacturer raises profit outlook as aviation recovery and data center boom drive turbine orders
articles.body:
Rolls-Royce Holdings Plc has unveiled its largest-ever share buyback program and substantially increased its midyear earnings forecast, signaling that the storied British engineering firm is reaping outsized benefits from two of the global economy's most powerful tailwinds: resurgent air travel and the explosive growth of data center infrastructure.

The announcement, made on February 26, underscores a remarkable turnaround for a company that was brought to the brink during the pandemic-era collapse in commercial aviation. Rolls-Royce, which earns a significant portion of its revenue from long-term service agreements tied to the flying hours of its wide-body jet engines, has seen those income streams recover sharply as international air traffic returns to and exceeds pre-pandemic levels. At the same time, the company's power systems division — which manufactures gas turbines and backup generators — has experienced a surge in orders driven by the insatiable energy demands of artificial intelligence data centers worldwide.

The record buyback reflects the confidence of Chief Executive Tufan Erginbilgiç, who has overseen a sweeping restructuring since taking the helm in early 2023. His cost-cutting measures, combined with strengthening end markets, have transformed Rolls-Royce's balance sheet from one weighed down by debt into a position of sufficient strength to return substantial capital to shareholders. The raised profit guidance suggests management believes the favorable demand environment has further room to run.

For investors, the dual growth engines powering Rolls-Royce's performance are particularly appealing because they diversify the company's exposure beyond its traditional aerospace heartland. The data center opportunity, in particular, represents a secular growth story that could sustain elevated demand for power systems well beyond the current cycle. Analysts have noted that hyperscale cloud providers and AI firms are racing to secure reliable power generation capacity, placing Rolls-Royce in a favorable competitive position.

Rolls-Royce shares have already been among the top performers on the London Stock Exchange over the past two years, and the latest announcement is likely to reinforce the bullish consensus. As the UK's largest manufacturer charts a course toward sustained profitability, the combination of aerospace recovery and energy infrastructure demand has positioned the company as one of Britain's most compelling industrial success stories.
